title: Interview Templates
description: Drill the most common algorithmic templates that appear in coding interviews. Each pattern provides a reusable TypeScript skeleton you can adapt to dozens of problems, so recognizing and applying them quickly becomes second nature.

sections.title: Sliding Window
sections.explanation: The sliding window technique maintains a fixed- or variable-size window over a sequence, sliding it forward one element at a time. Instead of recalculating from scratch, you update the result by subtracting the element leaving the window and adding the one entering. This brings many brute-force O(n*k) problems down to O(n), which interviewers expect you to recognize immediately.

exercise.prompt: Complete the sliding window function to find the maximum sum of any contiguous subarray of size k. Print the result for nums = [2, 3, 1, 5, 4, 2] with k = 3.

validation.message: Maximum sum subarray of size 3 is [1,5,4] = 10... wait, [3,1,5]=9, [1,5,4]=10, [5,4,2]=11. Answer is 11.
Subtract the element leaving the window and use Math.max to track the best

exercise.hint: Subtract the element leaving the window with nums[i - k] and use Math.max(best, window) to track the largest sum.

sections.title: Binary Search
sections.explanation: Binary search halves the search space each iteration by comparing the middle element to the target. The template uses two pointers, left and right, with a `while (left <= right)` loop. TypeScript's number type handles both the indices and the comparison naturally. Interviewers test edge cases like off-by-one errors and the choice of mid calculation.

exercise.prompt: Complete the binary search function to find the index of target in a sorted array. Return -1 if not found. Print the result for nums = [2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12] and target = 10.

validation.message: 10 is at index 4 in the sorted array
Use <= for the loop condition and adjust by 1 to avoid infinite loops

exercise.hint: The loop runs while left <= right. When the middle value is too small, move left to mid + 1; when too large, move right to mid - 1.

sections.title: Two Pointers
sections.explanation: The two-pointer technique places one pointer at the start and one at the end of a sorted array, then moves them inward based on a condition. For a pair-sum problem, if the current sum is too small you advance the left pointer; if too large you retreat the right pointer. This solves the problem in O(n) time with O(1) space.

exercise.prompt: Complete the two-pointer function to find a pair of indices whose values sum to target in a sorted array. Print the result for nums = [1, 2, 4, 6, 8, 11] and target = 10.

validation.message: Move pointers inward by 1 and use typed tuple return

exercise.hint: When the sum is too small, increment left by 1 to increase it. When too large, decrement right by 1 to decrease it.

sections.title: Prefix Sum
sections.explanation: A prefix sum array stores the cumulative sum up to each index, turning any range-sum query into a constant-time subtraction. Building the prefix array is O(n), and each query is O(1). Interviewers use this pattern in subarray-sum problems, and it often serves as a building block inside more complex solutions.

exercise.prompt: Complete the prefix sum builder and use it to answer a range sum query. Print the sum of elements from index 1 to index 3 (inclusive) for nums = [2, 5, 1, 7, 3].

validation.message: Build prefix from previous entry and query with subtraction

exercise.hint: Each prefix entry builds on the previous one: prefix[i + 1] = prefix[i] + nums[i]. The range sum from l to r is prefix[r + 1] - prefix[l].

sections.title: Backtracking
sections.explanation: Backtracking explores every candidate solution by choosing an option, recursing deeper, then undoing the choice to try the next option. This choose-explore-unchoose pattern is the backbone of permutation, combination, and constraint-satisfaction problems. TypeScript's typed arrays keep the path and result type-safe throughout the recursion.

exercise.prompt: Complete the backtracking function to generate all permutations of [1, 2, 3]. Print each permutation as comma-separated values, one per line.

validation.message: Use typed backtracking with path copy and pop to undo choices

exercise.hint: Skip element i with remaining.slice(i + 1). Pop the last element from path to undo the choice before the next iteration.
